products.name: Ocellaris Clownfish
products.shortDescription: A hardy orange reef fish that's a great choice for newer saltwater keepers.
products.extendedDescription:
The ocellaris clownfish is one of the most popular aquarium fish for a reason. They're colorful, active, and usually adapt well to life in a home reef tank. They can live peacefully with many smaller reef species and are often kept with anemones, although an anemone is not required.

Care Notes:
• Minimum tank size: 20 gallons
• Temperature: 75-82°F
• Diet: Omnivore
• Difficulty: Beginner

Avoid keeping them with large aggressive fish that may see them as food.

products.name: Blue Hippo Tang
products.shortDescription: A bright blue reef favorite that needs plenty of swimming space.
products.extendedDescription:
The blue hippo tang is famous for its bright blue color and playful personality. While they are a popular choice for reef tanks, they are not ideal for small aquariums because they grow larger and spend a lot of time swimming. A spacious tank and good water quality are important for keeping them healthy.

Care Notes:
• Minimum tank size: 100 gallons recommended
• Temperature: 72-78°F
• Diet: Herbivore (mostly algae-based)
• Difficulty: Intermediate

They are generally peaceful but should have plenty of room to avoid stress.

products.name: Dwarf Seahorse
products.shortDescription: A tiny seahorse with unique behavior, but best suited for experienced keepers.
products.extendedDescription:
Dwarf seahorses are one of the smallest seahorse species available in the aquarium trade. Their small size and slow movement make them interesting to watch, but they require more specialized care than most fish. They do best in species-only tanks with calm water and a steady food supply.

Care Notes:
• Minimum tank size: 5-10 gallons
• Temperature: 72-78°F
• Diet: Small live foods such as baby brine shrimp
• Difficulty: Advanced

Avoid keeping them with fast or aggressive tank mates that can outcompete them for food.

products.name: Longhorn Cowfish
products.shortDescription: A unique boxfish with horns and a big personality, but not for beginner tanks.
products.extendedDescription:
The longhorn cowfish is one of the more unusual looking fish in the hobby, with its boxy shape and distinctive horns. They are peaceful but require careful tank planning because they can become stressed easily and may release toxins if severely threatened or injured.

Care Notes:
• Minimum tank size: 100 gallons recommended
• Temperature: 72-78°F
• Diet: Omnivore
• Difficulty: Advanced

Give them plenty of open swimming space and avoid housing them with aggressive fish.

products.name: Fiddler Ray
products.shortDescription: A striking bottom dwelling ray that requires a very large, specialized aquarium.
products.extendedDescription:
The fiddler ray is a beautiful bottom-dweller known for its unique markings and calm nature. They are not a typical home aquarium fish and need a lot of floor space, excellent filtration, and an experienced caretaker. Their care requirements make them better suited for advanced marine setups.

Care Notes:
• Minimum tank size: 180+ gallons recommended
• Temperature: 68-77°F
• Diet: Carnivore
• Difficulty: Expert

Provide a soft sandy substrate and avoid sharp decorations that could damage their underside.

products.name: Leafy Seadragon
products.shortDescription: A bizarre Australian relative of the seahorse covered in leaflike camouflage.
products.extendedDescription: Leafy seadragons are one of the strangest looking animals in the ocean, using their leaf shaped fins to blend into kelp and seaweed. They are peaceful but extremely delicate, making them a poor choice for most home aquariums. They need excellent water quality, a calm environment, and a steady supply of small foods like mysis shrimp.

products.name: Mandarinfish
products.shortDescription: A tiny reef fish with incredible colors and a reputation for being difficult to keep.
products.extendedDescription: Mandarinfish are famous for their bright blue, orange, and green patterns. They spend most of their time picking tiny organisms from live rock and sand, so they do best in mature reef tanks with plenty of natural food available. They are peaceful fish, but they can be picky eaters and are best kept by experienced aquarium owners.
